title: Federated Averaging (FedAvg)
description: Understand the canonical federated averaging algorithm, its implementation details and convergence properties.
content:
Federated Averaging (FedAvg) is the foundational algorithm in federated learning, introduced by McMahan et al. in their seminal paper 'Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data'.

Algorithm Overview

FedAvg works by iteratively:

  1. Selecting a subset of clients to participate in the current training round
  2. Sending the current global model to selected clients
  3. Having each client train the model on their local data for several epochs
  4. Aggregating the resulting model updates (typically by averaging)
  5. Updating the global model

Key Properties

  • Communication Efficiency: By performing multiple local training steps before communication, FedAvg reduces the communication overhead compared to distributed SGD.
  • Privacy Preservation: Raw data never leaves client devices, only model updates are shared.
  • Resilience to Unbalanced Data: FedAvg can handle situations where clients have different amounts of training data.

Mathematical Formulation

Let w be the global model parameters and w_i be the local model parameters for client i. The FedAvg update rule can be written as:

w_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{n_i}{n} w_i^t

Where n_i is the number of samples at client i, n is the total number of samples across all clients, and K is the number of clients participating in the current round.

Convergence Properties

FedAvg convergence depends on several factors:

  • The degree of non-IID data distribution among clients
  • The number of local epochs performed by each client
  • The fraction of clients participating in each round
  • The learning rate schedule

Under certain conditions on the loss function (e.g., convexity, smoothness) and with appropriate hyperparameter settings, FedAvg can be shown to converge to an optimal or near-optimal solution.

relatedPapers.title: Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data
relatedPapers.authors: McMahan, H. B., Moore, E., Ramage, D., Hampson, S., & Arcas, B. A.

relatedPapers.title: Federated Optimization in Heterogeneous Networks
relatedPapers.authors: Li, T., Sahu, A. K., Zaheer, M., Sanjabi, M., Talwalkar, A., & Smith, V.

codeExamples.title: FedAvg Implementation in PyTorch
codeExamples.code:
import torch

def federated_avg(global_model, client_models, client_data_sizes):
    """Perform federated averaging on client models
    
    Args:
        global_model: The global model to be updated
        client_models: List of client models after local training
        client_data_sizes: List of data sizes for each client
        
    Returns:
        Updated global model
    """
    # Get the total data size
    total_data_size = sum(client_data_sizes)
    
    # Initialize a dictionary to store the averaged parameters
    global_params = {}
    for name, param in global_model.named_parameters():
        global_params[name] = torch.zeros_like(param.data)
    
    # Perform weighted averaging of model parameters
    for client_idx, client_model in enumerate(client_models):
        weight = client_data_sizes[client_idx] / total_data_size
        for name, param in client_model.named_parameters():
            global_params[name] += param.data * weight
    
    # Update the global model parameters
    for name, param in global_model.named_parameters():
        param.data = global_params[name]
    
    return global_model

# Example client update function
def client_update(client_model, optimizer, train_loader, epochs):
    """Update client model on local data
    
    Args:
        client_model: Model to be updated
        optimizer: Optimizer for training
        train_loader: DataLoader for client's training data
        epochs: Number of local training epochs
        
    Returns:
        Updated client model
    """
    client_model.train()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        for data, target in train_loader:
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            output = client_model(data)
            loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(output, target)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
    
    return client_model

codeExamples.title: FedAvg Implementation in TensorFlow
codeExamples.code:
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

def federated_avg(global_weights, client_weights, client_data_sizes):
    """Perform federated averaging on client models
    
    Args:
        global_weights: List of Numpy arrays containing global model weights
        client_weights: List of lists of Numpy arrays containing client model weights
        client_data_sizes: List of data sizes for each client
        
    Returns:
        Updated global weights
    """
    # Get the total data size
    total_data_size = sum(client_data_sizes)
    
    # Initialize new global weights
    new_weights = [np.zeros_like(w) for w in global_weights]
    
    # Perform weighted averaging of model parameters
    for c_idx, c_weights in enumerate(client_weights):
        weight = client_data_sizes[c_idx] / total_data_size
        for i, w in enumerate(c_weights):
            new_weights[i] += w * weight
    
    return new_weights

# Example client update function
def client_update(model, dataset, epochs, batch_size):
    """Update client model on local data
    
    Args:
        model: TensorFlow model to be updated
        dataset: Client's dataset as (features, labels)
        epochs: Number of local training epochs
        batch_size: Batch size for training
        
    Returns:
        Updated model weights
    """
    features, labels = dataset
    num_samples = len(features)
    dataset = tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices(
        (features, labels)).shuffle(num_samples).batch(batch_size)
    
    for _ in range(epochs):
        for x, y in dataset:
            with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
                pred = model(x, training=True)
                loss = tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy()(y, pred)
            grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
            model.optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    
    return [w.numpy() for w in model.trainable_variables]
